summary: Gruber argues that Bloomberg's Mark Gurman dramatically overhyped a report about Apple's M4 chip plans, framing routine annual updates as a reactionary 'overhaul' driven by AI panic and declining Mac sales. He methodically lays out how Apple silicon has been settling into a predictable annual cadence since the M1 launch, and that everything in Gurman's report — updated Macs across the lineup, faster GPUs, bigger Neural Engines, more RAM — is the natural progression of the platform, not a strategic pivot. He pushes back on the narrative that Mac sales declined due to tepid M3 upgrades, attributing the dip instead to Covid-era laptop sales being pulled forward. The actual story, Gruber contends, is simply that Apple has successfully gotten its Mac lineup onto an annual refresh cycle.
key_points: Apple silicon has been settling into a predictable annual cadence: MacBook Pros in fall, Airs in early spring, desktops by WWDC — and M4 continuing this pattern is the real story
The decline in Mac sales is better explained by Covid-era purchase pull-forward and M1 Macs being good enough that owners have little reason to upgrade, not by M3 being a weak generation
Gurman's 'AI-focused' framing is superficial — faster GPUs and bigger Neural Engines are how Apple silicon has evolved for 15 years, and no one expects M4-exclusive AI features
Raising the Mac Pro memory ceiling from 192 GB to 512 GB is a natural platform progression, not a response to criticism
Bloomberg's incentive to frame routine product updates as dramatic pivots serves stock-market narratives more than accurate tech reporting
money_quotes: The M1 and M2 generations were like an airplane taking off — a bit rocky and rough. Turbulence is to be expected. But with the M3, Apple silicon hit cruising altitude.
This year, product rumors need only three things: a noun, a verb, and 'AI'.
Apple silicon was so good right out of the gate that most people who own M1 Macs — any M1 Macs, including the base MacBook Air and Mac Mini — have little reason to consider upgrading yet.
No one is postulating that M4-based Macs will offer AI features that require M4 chips.

That they will provide Apple with AI-related performance to brag about just means they'll have faster GPUs and bigger Neural Engines, which is exactly how Apple silicon has been evolving year-over-year for 15 years.

The actual story is 'the entire Mac product line is set for annual speed-bump Apple silicon updates' — not 'Mac sales are in the tank and Apple is overhauling the whole product line to change its focus to AI.'
key_insight: Bloomberg's breathless framing of routine annual Mac updates as an AI-driven overhaul says more about the media's AI hype cycle than about Apple's actual product strategy.
